articles.title: Hawaii home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI
articles.dek: Quarterly, annual, and longer-run state HPI comparisons with a clear boundary between market movement and property value.
articles.previewText: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for Hawaii and why an index cannot value a specific property.
articles.metaDescription: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for Hawaii and why an index cannot value a specific property.

sections.heading: Borrower view: what changed over the quarter and year
body: The local figures answer the first question: As of 2026 Q1, Hawaii's purchase-only HPI rose 2.9% over the latest quarter and rose 2.1% over the year. For the decision at hand, the quarterly window is more sensitive to recent movement, while the annual window compares the latest quarter with the matching quarter one year earlier. That difference may matter when a borrower checks whether search, offer, or refinance assumptions still fit the broad direction of the state market. In practical terms, if the two windows diverge, timing is part of the answer; if they align, local variation still remains. Neither change identifies a home's value, a competitive offer, an affordable payment, or what the index will do next, so current property and financing evidence must carry the decision.

sections.heading: Evidence check: place the latest movement in a longer sequence
body: Over the five years ending 2026 Q1, the Hawaii HPI rose 38.8%, compared with the latest annual movement of 2.1%. For borrower planning, the longer window shows how far the repeat-sale index moved across several market cycles, while the one-year figure gives more weight to recent conditions. That contrast may help a borrower recognize when an older search budget, equity assumption, or comparable sale needs fresh evidence. It does not justify applying the five-year percentage to an old purchase price, and it cannot forecast a future sale. Even when the long and short directions agree, current comparable properties, taxes, insurance, financing costs, condition, and a property-specific payment worksheet are still needed before the trend informs a mortgage decision.

sections.heading: The dated finding: the index level is context, not a home price
body: Hawaii's latest FHFA purchase-only HPI level is 398.7. For the decision at hand, that number is a benchmark for repeat-sale price movement, not a dollar-denominated typical home price, listing price, or appraisal.

For borrowers reviewing evidence from Hawaii, reading the 398.7 level with the 2.9% quarterly change keeps the index itself separate from its rate of movement. In practical terms, borrowers can apply the dated level to identify the release being discussed, but not to value a selected property. Local inventory, property type, condition, concessions, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association costs, and financing terms still need current evidence. This boundary is especially important when broad statewide movement differs from the neighborhood, price band, or property type involved in an actual purchase, refinance, or equity review.

sections.heading: Evidence limits: the HPI cannot value a property or set a payment
body: An HPI level of 398.7 cannot establish the dollar value of a property in Hawaii, and an annual movement of 2.1% cannot establish how much any home gained or lost. In practical terms, the index excludes the borrower's down payment, loan amount, interest rate, taxes, insurance, association costs, repairs, and time horizon. FHFA can also revise published values, so the 2.9% and 38.8% comparisons should retain their source period.

Applied to a decision in Hawaii, the 398.7 level must remain separate from the dollar figures used in a property review. Those limits make the state index useful for orientation but unsuitable as an appraisal, offer recommendation, equity calculation, promised appreciation claim, payment quote, or underwriting conclusion.

sections.heading: Evidence check: use the index to test local assumptions
body: A Hawaii search plan can apply the quarterly, annual, and five-year windows to ask whether current listings, seller concessions, and property types resemble the broad state movement. At an annual movement of 2.1%, the dated 398.7 index level provides a reference point for that question without supplying a sale price. If the windows point in different directions, the divergence shows why the selected period matters; if they point together, statewide consistency still does not erase local variation. In practical terms, the plan therefore should compare the broad pattern with current listings and recent property-level evidence instead of treating any percentage as a forecast. FHFA HPI measures repeat-sale movement, not the complete set of homes available to one borrower.

sections.heading: Borrower action: connect the state trend to current property evidence
body: A practical Hawaii borrower review can record 2.9%, 2.1%, and 38.8% as statewide trend markers, then compare them with current local listings and recent property-level evidence.

For borrowers reviewing evidence from Hawaii, for a purchase, keep the 2.9% state movement as context while adding the selected price, inspection findings, appraisal process, taxes, insurance, cash-to-close, and loan disclosures. For a refinance or equity question, begin with a defensible property review in place of multiplying a prior value by 2.1% or 38.8%. In practical terms, this approach preserves the information in the 398.7 HPI while giving current local and borrower-specific facts the decision-making role that a statewide repeat-sale index cannot fill.

articles.methodology: This article uses the official FHFA purchase-only state quarterly HPI and calculates changes from matching prior-quarter, prior-year, and five-year index observations when available. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: FHFA may revise index values, statewide movement can mask local and property-type differences, and an index level is not a dollar-denominated home value. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Hawaii statewide labor market: latest employment evidence
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest BLS labor-force, employment, unemployment, and rate measures for Hawaii.
articles.previewText: See what changed in Hawaii's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
articles.metaDescription: See what changed in Hawaii's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
keyTakeaways: change in unemployment rate from the prior month: 0.1 percentage points.
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier: 0.1 percentage points.

evidenceFacts.label: change in unemployment rate from the prior month
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier

sections.heading: Read the comparison: what changed in the latest month
body: The local figures answer the first question: As of May 2026, Hawaii's unemployment rate rose from 2.4% in April 2026 to 2.5% in May 2026, a change of 0.1 percentage points. For borrower planning, that movement may matter when a borrower is pressure-testing timing, reserves, or the documentation needed after a job or income change. It does not reveal that a particular employer is hiring or cutting jobs, and it does not establish one applicant's income stability, mortgage eligibility, or loan price. The release also estimates 17,470 people unemployed within a labor force of 689,589, which shows the scale of the movement without turning a state-level statistic into a borrower-level conclusion. Because LAUS values can be revised, keep the May 2026 source period attached to the comparison and verify household records separately.

sections.heading: What the comparison adds: what changed over the year
body: Over the year ending May 2026, Hawaii's unemployment rate rose from 2.4% in May 2025 to 2.5%, a change of 0.1 percentage points.

For borrower planning, that wider window helps show whether Hawaii's latest monthly movement sits inside a longer pattern, while the one-month movement of 0.1 percentage points answers a shorter timing question. Neither comparison predicts the next labor-market release for Hawaii or describes the stability of one household's earnings. For mortgage planning in Hawaii, the practical consequence is to date broad economic assumptions and then return to current pay history, variable compensation, self-employment records, contracts, leave, job changes, assets, and debts. Those borrower records carry more weight in a loan review than movement in Hawaii's broad labor-market estimates.

sections.heading: The dated finding: the counts behind the published rate
body: For the May 2026 source period, BLS estimated 689,589 people in the Hawaii labor force, including 672,119 employed and 17,470 unemployed under LAUS definitions. For the decision at hand, the employment and unemployment counts belong together because they establish the scale behind the published 2.5% rate. In mortgage planning, that state-level labor force describes the surrounding economy in place of the income or employment record of an applicant. In practical terms, it may help a borrower understand the size of the measured market, but qualification still depends on documented income, employment continuity, debts, assets, property facts, and the rules of the loan being considered. The counts should, as a result, follow the change summary instead of replacing the borrower answer.

sections.heading: Borrower view: why the rate needs its underlying counts
body: At 2.5%, Hawaii's unemployment rate summarizes 17,470 unemployed people within a labor force of 689,589; the rate should not be discussed without those counts.

For the next comparison in Hawaii, the 672,119 employment estimate is an economic measure and does not distinguish borrowers by hours, tenure, pay structure, industry, or the documentation available for a loan file. That makes 672,119 useful for local context but unsuitable as evidence that one household's income is stable or sufficient. For borrower planning, keeping the 2.5% rate attached to 689,589, 672,119, and 17,470 prevents a small percentage movement from carrying more borrower-level meaning than the LAUS release supports.

sections.heading: Labor statistics are not an income review
body: Neither 672,119 employed people nor an unemployment rate of 2.5% tells a lender what an applicant in Hawaii earns, whether that income is eligible, or whether it is likely to continue.

In a borrower review centered on Hawaii, the monthly movement of 0.1 percentage points also cannot substitute for pay stubs, tax returns, business records, contracts, benefit documentation, or an explanation of a recent job transition. The 2.5% rate can be relevant to timing and local economic questions while remaining outside the underwriting decision. That boundary protects borrowers from treating the year-over-year movement of 0.1 percentage points in either direction as an approval signal or as a conclusion about their own documented capacity.

sections.heading: Next step: turn the trend into documentation questions
body: A practical next step for borrowers in Hawaii is to note the latest 2.5% unemployment rate and the year-over-year movement of 0.1 percentage points, then move immediately to the records that describe the household.

After noting the 2.5% rate, compare current base pay, variable earnings, self-employment history, contracts, assets, debts, and expected changes with the documentation requirements for the loan path. The 689,589 local labor force and 672,119 employment count can frame the economic conversation, but they should not alter verified borrower inputs. For borrower planning, the monthly movement of 0.1 percentage points remains a population measure even when it appears consistent with a borrower's personal experience. A licensed professional can explain which documents and time periods matter while keeping Hawaii's 2.5% LAUS rate in its proper role as dated local economic context.

articles.methodology: This article uses BLS LAUS state monthly series for labor force, employment, unemployment, and unemployment rate, with prior-month and year-ago comparisons. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: LAUS estimates can be revised, city series are unadjusted, and local labor statistics describe a population rather than an individual applicant's income or employment. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Hawaii housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS
articles.dek: Statewide evidence on home value, income, owner costs, housing stock, tenure, vacancy, and rent for borrower planning.
articles.previewText: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for Hawaii with survey limitations and practical questions.
articles.metaDescription: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for Hawaii with survey limitations and practical questions.
keyTakeaways: As of: 2024 ACS 5-year estimates. Source period: 2024.
prior ACS state median home value: $615,300.

evidenceFacts.label: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage

sections.heading: Statewide value, income, and owner costs
body: Hawaii's 2024 ACS housing baseline reports a median owner-occupied home value of $839,100, median household income of $100,389, and median selected monthly owner costs of $2,904 for owners with a mortgage. In practical terms, those three reference points can still help a borrower identify which statewide value, income, and recurring-cost assumptions need local replacement first. They remain separate medians, not the price, income, and monthly budget of one typical household, and the $2,904 measure is not a real-estate-tax figure or a current mortgage quote. For the decision at hand, a real decision still requires local property evidence, borrower documentation, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, and current loan information.

sections.heading: Borrower view: how occupied housing is divided by tenure
body: Across Hawaii's measured housing stock of 567,896 units, the owner share of occupied housing is 63.0% and the renter share is 37.0%, making owner-occupied housing the larger statewide tenure group. In practical terms, the 63.0% and 37.0% shares describe how occupied units were distributed during the survey period; they do not measure current buyer demand, owner willingness to sell, or renter readiness to purchase.

The 63.0% and 37.0% statewide tenure split is useful for understanding scale and checking assumptions about the housing mix. For the decision at hand, it remains too broad to select a product or characterize a particular city, county, property type, or household inside Hawaii.

sections.heading: Borrower view: vacancy is broader than homes for sale
body: Hawaii's vacant-unit share is 13.3% within a statewide stock of 567,896 housing units. For the decision at hand, ACS vacancy includes categories beyond property currently offered for sale or rent, so 13.3% is not a live inventory rate and cannot measure competition for a selected home. Read beside the 63.0% owner share and 37.0% renter share, the vacancy figure helps describe how the broad stock was classified without predicting near-term supply. In practical terms, a borrower should use current listings, property condition, days on market, concessions, and local professional context to answer transaction questions that 13.3% and 567,896 were not designed to resolve.

sections.heading: Keep owner costs and rent comparable
body: The same Hawaii release reports $1,971 in median gross rent alongside $2,904 in median selected monthly owner costs for owners with a mortgage. For the decision at hand, the two measures cover different housing populations and cost definitions, so $1,971 should not be compared with only principal and interest or used to declare a statewide break-even point. A useful comparison replaces the $1,971 and $2,904 medians with a current lease and a current property budget, including utilities, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, transaction costs, and the expected time horizon. In practical terms, the $100,389 statewide median can provide economic context, but dividing it by either $1,971 or $2,904 would still combine medians that do not represent the same household.

sections.heading: Keep the statewide ACS vintages separate
body: The 2019 ACS published $615,300 as Hawaii's median owner-occupied home-value estimate, while the 2024 ACS published $839,100. For borrower planning, these nominal statewide observations may help show why an older planning reference needs current local evidence.

In a borrower review centered on Hawaii, they are not inflation-adjusted, and the ACS home-value methodology changed between the vintages, so the two dollar figures are not a like-for-like appreciation series and should not be converted into a precise percentage change. Both values summarize multi-year survey windows and carry sampling error. Keep $615,300 attached to 2019 and $839,100 attached to 2024, then use current local and property-specific evidence for purchase, refinance, or equity assumptions.

sections.heading: Next step: move from statewide context to a local worksheet
body: For a decision in Hawaii, a borrower may carry $839,100, $2,904, $1,971, and the 13.3% vacancy share into a local comparison as dated statewide context, then replace each with evidence matched to the actual decision. For the ownership budget, replace the $839,100 and $2,904 medians with a selected price, down payment, current loan disclosures, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, and cash reserves. On the rental side, replace the $1,971 median with a comparable lease, utilities, deposits, and expected changes over the same horizon.

For the decision at hand, the 37.0% renter share is useful for describing the statewide tenure mix, not for predicting which local option will be available. The 63.0% tenure measure may help explain the statewide housing mix, but it cannot decide whether buying, renting, refinancing, or using equity is suitable for one Hawaii household.

data.label: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage

rows: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 ACS 5-year state estimates for home value, household income, selected owner costs, rent, total and occupied housing units, vacancy, and owner and renter tenure, plus a separate 2019 nominal home-value observation. It does not calculate a percentage change between home-value vintages. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: ACS estimates cover multi-year survey periods, carry margins of error, and do not measure current listings, current financing terms, or a specific household's housing costs. The 2019 and 2024 home-value dollars are not inflation-adjusted, and their methodology is not directly comparable. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Hawaii 2026 county loan-limit landscape
articles.dek: How conforming and FHA one-unit limits vary across Hawaii counties and how borrowers can use them responsibly.
articles.previewText: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across Hawaii without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
articles.metaDescription: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across Hawaii without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
sourceLabels: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
keyTakeaways: counties in the statewide limit summary: 5.

evidenceFacts.label: counties in the statewide limit summary

sections.heading: The dated finding: the conforming range across county records
body: The published evidence provides the starting point: Hawaii's statewide summary covers 5 county or county-equivalent records. For the decision at hand, within those records, the 2026 one-unit conforming limit runs from $1,249,125 to $1,299,500. That range may help borrowers see why the property county must be identified before a loan amount is assigned a conforming or jumbo category. In practical terms, the $1,249,125 to $1,299,500 range confirms that one conforming ceiling does not apply uniformly across Hawaii. The 5 record count keeps the range tied to the statewide source coverage rather than an assumed national rule. The $1,249,125 to $1,299,500 amounts classify loan size within the published program table; they do not establish a borrower's capacity, a property's value, or the price of financing. Before using the $1,299,500 statewide maximum, county, legal unit count, and expected base loan amount must be known.

sections.heading: Why county verification matters
body: 2 Hawaii counties sit above the conforming baseline in this summary, while the full conforming range reaches from $1,249,125 to $1,299,500. For the decision at hand, that count shows where a statewide shortcut can fail: a property in a higher-limit county may use a different published ceiling than one at $1,249,125. The 2 figure does not mean those counties are more affordable, more desirable, or more likely to approve a loan. In practical terms, it only describes how many matched county rows exceed the baseline. The $1,299,500 maximum therefore should be treated as evidence that county lookup matters, not as the default Hawaii limit. Before treating $1,299,500 as relevant, a borrower should identify the property's official county or county-equivalent area and then attach conventional, jumbo, or FHA labels to the desired loan amount.

sections.heading: Read the comparison: conforming and FHA ranges answer different questions
body: The highest one-unit conforming amount in Hawaii is $1,299,500, while the highest FHA amount is $1,299,500; at the low end, the figures are $1,249,125 and $586,500.

For the next comparison in Hawaii, these pairs show program boundaries, not competing offers. A requested amount below $1,299,500 may still fall outside the applicable county's conforming limit, and an amount below $1,299,500 may still face FHA borrower and property requirements. For borrower planning, comparing $1,249,125, $1,299,500, $586,500, and $1,299,500 is useful only when county and unit count are held constant. The $1,299,500 and $1,299,500 ceilings leave rates, fees, mortgage insurance, appraisal, reserves, debt capacity, and product suitability as separate questions for the documented transaction.

sections.heading: The FHA range follows its own county table
body: Across the same 5 Hawaii records, the 2026 one-unit FHA limit spans $586,500 to $1,299,500.

For the next comparison in Hawaii, the $586,500 low and $1,299,500 high show that FHA's published boundary also varies within the state. The $586,500 floor and $1,299,500 high remain county-table values even when they match conforming amounts elsewhere. For borrower planning, the FHA range should not be merged with the $1,249,125 to $1,299,500 conforming range, because the programs use different authorities and requirements. A $586,500 to $1,299,500 comparison can route loan-amount and product-category questions, but it cannot decide FHA eligibility, mortgage insurance, appraisal acceptability, occupancy compliance, cash needs, or approval.

sections.heading: Keep the boundary clear: a larger county limit is not greater affordability
body: A $1,299,500 conforming ceiling or $1,299,500 FHA ceiling does not make that loan amount affordable to a household in Hawaii, and the lower $1,249,125 or $586,500 figures do not describe home prices.

In a borrower review centered on Hawaii, published $1,299,500 and $1,299,500 limits does not establish borrower income, debts, credit, assets, occupancy, property condition, appraisal, insurance, reserves, rates, fees, or final approval. The $1,299,500 and $1,299,500 figures can also change with a new calendar year or corrected property geography. Keeping the 5 county-record scope and 2026 period attached to the figures prevents the statewide summary from being mistaken for a personalized product recommendation or a single amount that applies everywhere.

sections.heading: Borrower action: use the range to reach the correct county row
body: A useful next step is to verify the selected property's Hawaii county or county-equivalent area, legal unit count, price, down-payment plan, and expected base loan amount. For the decision at hand, then compare that amount with the exact official row, using the $1,249,125 to $1,299,500 conforming range and $586,500 to $1,299,500 FHA range only as orientation. The 2 above-baseline count explains why this lookup matters, but it does not replace it.

For borrowers reviewing evidence from Hawaii, keep the selected county row with the worksheet so the $1,299,500 and $1,299,500 statewide maximums cannot be mistaken for property-specific figures. After the 2 count prompts that county lookup, a licensed professional can explain current limits and borrower, property, mortgage-insurance, and documentation rules without presenting the largest statewide figure as available borrowing power.

articles.methodology: This article joins official 2026 FHFA conforming and HUD FHA county files by five-digit county FIPS, then summarizes the observed one-unit range and county counts for the state. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: State summaries can conceal special exceptions and multi-unit differences, and official program updates or property-location corrections can change the applicable row. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.
